title: Define application/bolyra-proof+cbor content type and envelope format
description: The protocol lacks a standardized wire format for proof exchange. Define an IANA-registrable media type application/bolyra-proof+cbor with a CDDL schema covering the three proof types (human, agent, delegation). The envelope should include: proof bytes, public signals array, circuit identifier, proving system tag (groth16/plonk), and a version field. This enables interop between implementations without out-of-band coordination on serialization. Artifact: spec/proof-envelope.cddl + updates to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md Section 6.

title: Complete DID resolution algorithm with verification method binding
description: spec/did-method-bolyra.md defines did:bolyra but lacks a concrete resolution algorithm mapping on-chain state to DID Documents. Specify: (1) how humanMerkleRoot and agentMerkleRoot map to verification methods, (2) the service endpoint for proof submission, (3) deactivation semantics when a nullifier is revoked. Without this, no universal resolver can implement did:bolyra. Artifact: updated spec/did-method-bolyra.md with Resolution, Deactivation, and Security Considerations sections per W3C DID Core §7-8.

title: Off-chain session token with on-chain checkpoint batching
description: After a successful handshake, emit a signed session token (JWT/SD-JWT with the nullifier, scopeCommitment, and expiry embedded) that parties can verify off-chain for subsequent calls within the session window. Batch session roots on-chain every N minutes. This eliminates per-call proving cost — the single biggest adoption blocker for agentic commerce where agents make dozens of tool calls per minute. Implement in the TS SDK as `createSessionToken()` and `verifySessionToken()`, with a new `SessionCheckpoint.sol` contract that accepts Merkle roots of verified sessions.

title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors for all three circuits
description: No independent implementation can verify compatibility without standardized test vectors. Produce a spec/test-vectors/ directory with JSON files covering: valid handshake (human+agent), expired credential rejection, stale root rejection, scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violation, delegation chain (1-hop and 3-hop), and nonce replay. Each vector includes input signals, expected outputs, and expected pass/fail. This is the minimum bar for any second implementation to claim conformance.

title: Specify human tree root history buffer matching agent tree
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but none for humanTree. The contract comment says 'humanTree uses Semaphore's' but the deployed code uses a standalone LeanIMT with no history. Any new human enrollment immediately invalidates in-flight proofs. Add a parallel ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE buffer for humanTree roots in the registry, and update the IETF draft's Security Considerations to document the staleness window. This is a protocol-level correctness gap, not just an implementation detail.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment to prevent bitmask enumeration
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) in AgentPolicy.circom uses only two inputs. Since permissionBitmask is 8 meaningful bits (256 values), an observer can brute-force all possible commitments against a known credentialCommitment to recover the exact permission set. Add a random blinding salt as a third Poseidon input: Poseidon3(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt). The salt becomes a private input; the commitment remains public. Update Delegation.circom to thread the salt through the chain. Document the enumeration attack and mitigation in the IETF draft §9 Security Considerations.

title: HumanUniqueness nullifier is constant across sessions, enabling cross-verifier linkability
description: The HumanUniqueness nullifier is `Poseidon2(scope, secret)` where scope is a constant like `hash('bolyra-handshake-v1')`. This means every handshake by the same human produces the identical nullifier, allowing colluding verifiers (or a single verifier across sessions) to link all activity to the same pseudonymous identity. The nonceBinding output prevents replay but does NOT prevent linkability. Fix: derive the nullifier as `Poseidon3(scope, secret, sessionNonce)` so each session produces a unique nullifier. Then use a separate `externalNullifier = Poseidon2(scope, secret)` checked on-chain only for revocation/sybil gating, not exposed to the verifier. This preserves sybil resistance while breaking cross-session linkability.

title: Add domain separation tags to all nullifier hash computations
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret) and AgentPolicy computes it as Poseidon2(secret, sessionNonce). Both use Poseidon2 with no domain separator, meaning a collision is possible if an agent's (secret, sessionNonce) pair equals a human's (scope, secret) pair — the nullifier spaces overlap. Prepend a unique domain tag constant (e.g., 0xHUMAN, 0xAGENT, 0xDELEG) as the first Poseidon input to each nullifier computation across all three circuits. This is a standard formal verification finding: hash function domain separation prevents cross-protocol nullifier confusion.
